38B
Cervical Spine, Hyoid, and Basicranium
Overview Cervical spine Hyoid Basicranium Rapid review
Objective 38B

Structure and development of the cervical spine, hyoid, and cranial base

This page brings together the anatomy and embryology you need for the cervical vertebrae, the hyoid bone, and the basicranium. The big pattern is that these regions are composite structures: they are built from distinct developmental pieces that later fuse into the anatomy you recognize in the adult.

Composite cranial bones: sphenoid, occipital, temporal
C1 and C2 are specialized cervical vertebrae
Dens belongs to C2, but develops from C1
Hyoid derives from 2nd and 3rd arches
High-yield snapshot

Learn the adult structure, then anchor each region to its developmental origin.

Cervical spine
C1-C7

Atlas, axis, then typical cervical vertebrae with transverse foramina and usually bifid spinous processes.

Hyoid
U-shaped

Suspended in the anterior neck, does not articulate with another bone, and helps keep the airway open.

Cranial base
3 fossae

The internal cranial base forms three deep fossae and includes the basicranial platform under the brainstem.

Cervical spine: structure first, development second

The cervical spine is easiest to organize as C1, C2, and C3-C7. C1 and C2 are specialized for supporting and rotating the head, while the remaining cervical vertebrae share a more typical vertebral plan.

C1 (atlas)

C1 is a ring-like vertebra that lacks a typical vertebral body. It is built from 2 lateral masses, 2 arches, and 2 transverse processes, and it articulates with the occipital condyles to support the skull.

  • Each transverse process splits into an anterior costal and posterior transverse part.
  • The atlas is specialized for nodding and for receiving the dens of C2.
  • Its body is not retained as an adult body of C1; embryologically, that body becomes part of C2.
Memory hook: atlas is the ring that carries the head.

C2 (axis)

C2 has a body with two lateral masses and the dens (odontoid process), which projects upward into the anterior part of the arch of C1 and functions as the pivot that C1 rotates around.

  • The dens is derived from the body of C1 during development.
  • The laminae unite posteriorly to form the spinous process.
  • Its transverse processes still separate into costal and transverse elements.
Clinical-style pearl: the atlantoaxial joint is the key rotation joint of the upper cervical region.

C3-C7 and cervical development

The remaining cervical vertebrae have small vertebral bodies, transverse foramina, and usually bifid spinous processes (except C7, the vertebra prominens).

  • Pedicles help form the intervertebral canal.
  • Laminae extend posteromedially and meet in the midline around the vertebral canal.
  • The posterior transverse element is larger than the anterior costal element.
  • Zygapophyseal joints are the articulations between adjacent vertebrae.
Embryology
  • The vertebrae arise from sclerotomes of paraxial mesoderm (somites).
  • The notochord contributes to the intervertebral discs.
  • The dens is part of C2 in the adult, but comes from C1 embryologically.

Hyoid bone: anatomy in the neck, arches in development

The hyoid is a unique bone because it is suspended rather than directly articulated. It acts as a stable anchoring point for muscles of the tongue, pharynx, and larynx.

Adult structure and function

The hyoid is a U-shaped bone in the anterior neck at about the level of C3-C4, in the angle between the mandible and thyroid cartilage. Its major job is to provide muscle attachment and help maintain an open airway.

  • It does not articulate with any other bone.
  • It is suspended by muscles and ligaments and connected functionally to the mandible, styloid processes, thyroid cartilage, manubrium, and scapula.
  • It is suspended from the styloid processes by the stylohyoid ligaments.

Parts of the hyoid

  • Body: central part; anterior surface projects anterosuperiorly and posterior surface projects posteroinferiorly.
  • Greater horns (greater cornua): extend posterosuperiorly and laterally from the body.
  • Lesser horns (lesser cornua): small projections near the junction of body and greater horn; may remain partly cartilaginous.
Age-related detail: the greater horns are joined to the body by fibrocartilage in youth and are commonly united by bone in older adults.

Development

The hyoid develops from the second and third pharyngeal arches.

Adult partEmbryologic source
Lesser horns + upper part of bodySecond arch (Reichert's cartilage)
Greater horns + lower part of bodyThird arch
Takeaway: one adult bone, but two arch contributions.

Basicranium and general cranial base organization

The basicranium is the platform that separates the bulbous neurocranium from the viscerocranium. It is not a single simple bone; instead it is built from contributions that become the composite cranial bones of the base.

Core idea: the cranial base has a roughened external surface for muscle attachment, an internal surface that forms three deep cranial fossae, a pharyngeal attachment below, and an upper surface that supports the brainstem and brain.
What makes up the basicranial platform?
Region / boneKey structural pointHigh-yield development point
Sphenoid Has a thickened midline basisphenoid, major foramina, and pterygoid plates. A cranial base composite bone with major neural crest contribution.
Occipital Has the basiocciput and is organized around the foramen magnum into squamous, lateral, and basilar parts. Derived largely from paraxial mesoderm (somites).
Temporal Includes the petrous portion, squamous portion, zygomatic process, styloid process, and tympanic part. Receives both neural crest and mesoderm contributions.
The three composite cranial bones

Sphenoid

The central keystone of the cranial base.

  • Includes the basisphenoid.
  • Superior orbital fissure transmits CN III, IV, VI, and V1.
  • Foramen rotundum transmits V2.
  • Pterygoid plates descend from the greater wing region.

Occipital

Frames the posterior cranial base and foramen magnum.

  • Divided into squamous, lateral, and basilar parts.
  • Occipital condyles sit lateral to the foramen magnum.
  • Supports articulation with C1 via the occipital condyles.

Temporal

A laterally placed composite bone with an especially important petrous part.

  • The petrous portion houses the cochlea and labyrinth.
  • The zygomatic process projects anteriorly.
  • The styloid process projects inferiorly and anteriorly.

Developmental pattern of the cranial base

The cranial base is developmentally mixed. That is why this region feels “composite” both anatomically and embryologically.

BoneMain origin noted here
OccipitalParaxial mesoderm (somites)
SphenoidNeural crest cells
TemporalBoth neural crest and mesoderm
Big picture: the cranial base is not built from one embryologic source.

General cranial base structure

  • Externally, the cranial base is roughened for muscle attachment.
  • Internally, it is organized into three deep cranial fossae.
  • Inferiorly, the pharynx and associated musculature attach to the underside of the base.
  • Superiorly, the brainstem rests on the upper surface of the basicranium.

Rapid review

Use this as a final pass right before a quiz or lab practical.

C1 Atlas = ring-like vertebra with lateral masses and arches; no typical vertebral body.
C2 Axis = vertebra with the dens, the pivot for rotation of the atlas and head.
Dens embryology Adult part of C2, but derived from the body of C1.
Typical cervical vertebrae Small bodies, transverse foramina, and usually bifid spinous processes; C7 is vertebra prominens.
Hyoid U-shaped, suspended, no bony articulation, and important for tongue/pharynx/larynx muscle attachment.
Hyoid development Lesser horns + upper body from 2nd arch; greater horns + lower body from 3rd arch.
Composite cranial bones Sphenoid, occipital, and temporal are the key composite bones emphasized here.
Cranial base Internal surface forms three deep cranial fossae; external surface is roughened for attachment.
